attrs.alt: Risky Users: risk aggregation from detections through to user risk level, CA policy trigger, and remediation paths

content.text: User Risk vs Sign-in Risk

Microsoft Entra ID Protection tracks two distinct risk dimensions. Sign-in risk assesses the suspicious characteristics of a single authentication event and resets when the event is resolved. User risk aggregates evidence of account-level compromise over time and persists until the account is remediated or an admin takes action.
Dimension
Sign-in Risk
User Risk
Scope
A single authentication event
The user account over time
Lifetime
Expires when the sign-in session ends
Persists until remediated, dismissed, or confirmed
What raises it
Suspicious characteristics of one sign-in (anonymous IP, AiTM, etc.)
Account-level signals: leaked creds, multiple risky sign-ins, threat intel
CA condition
signInRiskLevels (evaluated at token issuance)
userRiskLevels (evaluated at sign-in; persists across sessions)
Remediation
CA MFA challenge; admin confirms safe or compromised
SSPR via CA; admin dismisses or confirms compromised
Risk resets to None
After admin confirms safe or sign-in risk policy satisfied
After SSPR + admin confirms password changed; or admin dismisses

A user can have zero sign-in risk (all recent sign-ins look normal) but still carry high user risk if leaked credentials were found on the dark web. User risk accumulates from detections across all sign-ins, not just the most recent one.
Reading Risky Users

The riskyUsers endpoint returns all users whose current user risk level is above 'none'. Each record includes the risk level, state, detail code, and when the risk was last updated. The risk history endpoint shows how the risk evolved over time, including each detection that contributed to the current level.
Risk Detail Codes

The 'riskDetail' field on a risky user record explains why the risk is in its current state — whether it was machine-set, user-remediated, or admin-actioned. Use this field to triage efficiently and avoid re-investigating already-resolved events.
RiskDetail Value
Meaning
Typical Next Step
adminConfirmedUserCompromised
Admin manually set as compromised
Review admin action log; re-enable if cleared
userPerformedSecuredPasswordChange
User completed SSPR via risk policy
Risk auto-set to remediated; no action needed
aiConfirmedSigninSafe
Identity Protection ML determined sign-in was safe
No action — informational
leakedCredentials
Credentials found in dark web breach data
Force password reset immediately; check for lateral movement
userPassedMFADrivenByRiskBasedPolicy
User satisfied MFA challenge from risk CA policy
Sign-in risk lowered; monitor for repeat detections
adminDismissedAllRiskySigninsForUser
Admin cleared all sign-in risk events for user
Document justification for audit trail
none
No specific detail available (typical for ML-based detections)
Investigate recent sign-ins for context
Remediating and Dismissing Risky Users

Admins can take two manual actions on risky users: confirm compromised (high confidence, forces account disable and session revocation) or dismiss (false positive, marks risk as resolved without enforcing remediation). Dismissal does not change the user's password or revoke sessions.

# Confirm a user as compromised (triggers forced sign-out and disables account)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProtection/riskyUsers/confirmCompromised' \
    -Body (@{ userIds = @('<object-id-1>', '<object-id-2>') } | ConvertTo-Json) \
    -ContentType 'application/json'

# After investigation, re-enable the account and force password reset
Update-MgUser -UserId '<object-id>' -AccountEnabled $true
# User must reset password via SSPR or admin-set new password

# Dismiss risk for a user (false positive — marks as confirmedSafe)
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProtection/riskyUsers/dismiss' \
    -Body (@{ userIds = @('<object-id>') } | ConvertTo-Json) \
    -ContentType 'application/json'

# Bulk dismiss all medium-risk users (use with caution — verify these are FPs first)
$mediumRisk = Get-MgRiskyUser -Filter "riskLevel eq 'medium' and riskState eq 'atRisk'"
$ids = $mediumRisk | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Id
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identityProtection/riskyUsers/dismiss' \
    -Body (@{ userIds = $ids } | ConvertTo-Json) \
    -ContentType 'application/json'
Connecting User Risk to Conditional Access

The recommended automated remediation path is a CA policy with userRiskLevels as a condition and 'passwordChange' in the grant controls. When the user satisfies SSPR, Identity Protection automatically resets the user risk to 'remediated'. SSPR must be configured and enabled before deploying this policy.
Test SSPR registration status before enabling the policy: if users have not registered SSPR methods, they will be unable to satisfy the grant control and will be effectively locked out.
Exclude break-glass and emergency access accounts from this policy — those accounts should not be blocked even at high user risk.
For guest accounts, SSPR from the resource tenant is usually not available — exclude guests and handle their risk through a separate process.
Automated Response with Logic Apps

For organizations that need faster response than CA policies provide (e.g., disabling accounts within seconds of a detection), Microsoft Sentinel playbooks (Logic Apps) can automate the full incident response workflow triggered by AADRiskyUsers alerts.

# Sentinel / Logic App playbook pattern for automated response to high user risk
# Trigger: Microsoft Sentinel alert from AADRiskyUsers high-risk detection
# Actions:
#   1. Get user details from Entra ID
#   2. Disable the account immediately
#   3. Revoke all active sessions
#   4. Post a Teams alert to the SOC channel
#   5. Create an incident ticket in ServiceNow

# PowerShell equivalent of steps 2-3 (usable in Azure Automation Runbook):
param([string]$UserId)

# Step 2: Disable the account
Update-MgUser -UserId $UserId -AccountEnabled $false
Write-Host "Account disabled: $UserId"

# Step 3: Revoke all active refresh tokens (signs out all sessions immediately)
Invoke-MgUserRevokeSignInSession -UserId $UserId
Write-Host "Sessions revoked: $UserId"

# Step 4: Send Teams notification (Teams webhook or Graph send message)
$teamsPayload = @{
    text = "ALERT: User $UserId flagged as HIGH RISK. Account disabled. SOC review required."
} | ConvertTo-Json
Invoke-RestMethod -Method POST -Uri $env:TEAMS_WEBHOOK_URL -Body $teamsPayload -ContentType 'application/json'
KQL Monitoring
The AADRiskyUsers and AADUserRiskEvents tables in Log Analytics provide historical data for trend analysis, SLA tracking (how quickly risky users are remediated), and breach response coordination.

// KQL: risky user trend — new high-risk users per day
AADRiskyUsers
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(30d)
| where RiskLevel == 'high'
| where RiskState == 'atRisk'
| summarize NewHighRisk = dcount(UserPrincipalName) by bin(TimeGenerated, 1d)
| order by TimeGenerated asc

// KQL: users whose risk was dismissed vs confirmed compromised
AADRiskyUsers
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(90d)
| where RiskState in ('dismissed', 'confirmedCompromised')
| summarize Count = count() by RiskState, bin(TimeGenerated, 7d)
| order by TimeGenerated asc

// KQL: correlation — leaked credential detections
AADUserRiskEvents
| where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
| where RiskEventType == 'leakedCredentials'
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, RiskLevel, RiskState
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Time-to-Remediation SLA Tracking

Security teams typically set SLAs for how quickly high user risk must be investigated and remediated. Log Analytics KQL can measure the time between a user entering 'atRisk' state and reaching a terminal state (remediated, dismissed, or confirmedCompromised).

# Track time-to-remediation for risky users (SLA measurement)
# Correlate AADRiskyUsers state changes to measure how long accounts stay at high risk

// KQL: average hours from atRisk to remediated/dismissed (rolling 30d)
let created = AADRiskyUsers
    | where RiskState == 'atRisk'
    | project UserId, RiskedAt = TimeGenerated, RiskLevel;
let resolved = AADRiskyUsers
    | where RiskState in ('remediated', 'dismissed', 'confirmedCompromised')
    | project UserId, ResolvedAt = TimeGenerated, FinalState = RiskState;
created
| join kind=inner resolved on UserId
| where ResolvedAt > RiskedAt
| extend HoursToResolve = datetime_diff('hour', ResolvedAt, RiskedAt)
| summarize AvgHours = avg(HoursToResolve), P95Hours = percentile(HoursToResolve, 95)
    by RiskLevel, FinalState
| order by RiskLevel asc
Guest and External User Risk

Guest accounts appear in the risky users report when their sign-ins from the resource tenant trigger detections. Unlike member accounts, guests cannot complete SSPR in the resource tenant — the only self-remediation path is through their home tenant. For this reason, guest user risk requires a different response strategy.

Guest user risk CA policies should use 'block' rather than 'passwordChange' as the grant control, since guests cannot complete SSPR in the resource tenant. This is a hard block — contact the home tenant admin or remove guest access to resolve.
Break-Glass Account Exclusions

Break-glass accounts (emergency access accounts used when normal admin access fails) must be excluded from all user risk CA policies. A high user risk event could otherwise block the only emergency access path, creating an admin lockout during a security incident.

# Break-glass accounts should be EXCLUDED from user risk policies
# These accounts are used in emergencies when normal admin access fails;
# blocking them on high user risk could cause a complete admin lockout.

# Create an exclusion group for break-glass accounts
$bgGroup = New-MgGroup -DisplayName 'Break-Glass Exclusion' -MailEnabled $false \
    -SecurityEnabled $true -MailNickname 'BreakGlassExclusion'

# Add break-glass accounts to the group
New-MgGroupMember -GroupId $bgGroup.Id -DirectoryObjectId '<breakglass-user-object-id>'

# In the CA user risk policy, exclude this group:
# Conditions.Users.ExcludeGroups = @($bgGroup.Id)

# Monitor break-glass accounts separately via diagnostic logs.
# Any sign-in from a break-glass account should trigger an immediate SOC alert.
# KQL to detect break-glass usage:
// SigninLogs
// | where UserPrincipalName in ('breakglass1@contoso.com', 'breakglass2@contoso.com')
// | where TimeGenerated >= ago(7d)
// | project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, AppDisplayName, ResultType
Session Revocation After Compromise

Confirming a user as compromised does not automatically revoke tokens in all apps — it depends on whether those apps support Continuous Access Evaluation. For apps that do not support CAE, revokeSignInSessions forces a revocation that takes effect at the next token refresh. Disabling and re-enabling the account invalidates the refresh token immediately.

# After confirming compromise, revoke all active tokens for the user
# This signs out all active sessions across all apps immediately
Invoke-MgUserRevokeSignInSession -UserId '<object-id>'

# For Continuous Access Evaluation (CAE) capable apps,
# revocation propagates within ~15 seconds.
# For non-CAE apps, revocation takes effect at next token refresh (up to 1 hour).

# To force immediate re-authentication on non-CAE apps,
# also reset the user's refresh token by disabling and re-enabling the account:
Update-MgUser -UserId '<object-id>' -AccountEnabled $false
Start-Sleep -Seconds 2
Update-MgUser -UserId '<object-id>' -AccountEnabled $true
# Now set a temporary password or force SSPR before re-enabling full access

# Audit which apps had active sessions at time of compromise:
Get-MgAuditLogSignIn -Filter "userId eq '<object-id>' and createdDateTime ge 2026-06-01" -Top 50 |
    Select-Object AppDisplayName, CreatedDateTime, IPAddress, Status |
    Sort-Object CreatedDateTime -Descending
Cross-Tenant and Edge-Case Risk Scenarios
Scenario
Risk Behavior
Action
Guest user's home tenant detects leaked creds
Risk elevation may not propagate to resource tenant
Monitor resource tenant's own risky user report for the guest UPN
B2B user risks from external tenant
Resource tenant sees its own sign-in risk for the external user
Cross-tenant access settings can enforce MFA trust from home tenant
Workload identity risk (service principal)
Tracked separately under riskyServicePrincipals; not in riskyUsers
Review servicePrincipalRiskDetections endpoint
Shared device sign-ins
Multiple users on same device; risk may appear on each
Use device-based CA conditions, not user risk, for shared kiosks
Summary
User risk aggregates account-level compromise signals over time; sign-in risk covers individual authentication events.
The riskDetail field explains the current risk state — leakedCredentials, adminConfirmedUserCompromised, userPerformedSecuredPasswordChange, and others.
The primary automated remediation path is a CA user-risk policy that requires SSPR — completing SSPR auto-resets the risk to remediated.
Admin actions: dismiss (false positive, no remediation) or confirm compromised (forces disable + session revocation).
Exclude break-glass accounts and guests from user-risk CA policies to avoid lockouts.
Sentinel playbooks enable sub-minute automated response (disable + revoke + alert) for high user risk detections.
